segments.s474-g33: هم‌انگه is an adverbial phrase meaning “at that moment” placed at the clause start to give the time. باسپ اندر آورد پای is a compact, somewhat archaic verbal expression: آورد (past) plus the object پای (“foot”) means “set/put his foot” or “took a step”; اندر is the old preposition “in/into,” so اندر آورد پای literally “brought/put (his) foot in/forward.” باسپ here functions as an adverbial/phrase modifying the action (see senses note) and the whole segment describes a punctual physical movement immediately before the next action.
segments.s474-g34: به آواز مهران is a prepositional phrase where به + noun indicates “at/on the sound of” (literally “by the voice of Mehran”); مهران is the name in genitive relation. بیامد is the simple past ‘came/rose’ (3rd‑singular) indicating the action triggered by that sound. ز جای (z + جای) is a short adverbial meaning “from (his) place/seat,” placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s474-g35: حکیمان is the plural subject “the sages/wise men.” برفتند is the past plural verb “they went/they departed.” با او is the simple prepositional phrase “with him.” به هم is an adverbial meaning “together” (literally “to one another/together”) and here reinforces that the sages accompanied him as a group.
segments.s474-g36: بدان تا is a purposive connector: بدان (here functioning as ‘with that/in that (action)’) plus تا introduces the purpose clause “so that / in order that.” سپهبد is the subject of the subordinate clause. نباشد is the negative subjunctive/optative form of بودن used here to express the desired outcome (“would not be / should not be”), and دژم is an adjective “sullen/gloomy” predicated of the spahbed.
segments.s474-g37: جهاندار is the subject “the sovereign/king.” چون at clause-initial position functions temporally as “when/as” (classical use) introducing the time clause. نزد مهران is a prepositional/adpositional phrase meaning “in the presence of / near Mehran.” رسید is the past tense verb “arrived/reached.”
segments.s474-g38: بپرسید is past third‑person singular “he asked.” داننده را is the definite object marked by را: داننده (“the knower/sage/expert”) is whom he questioned. چون here means “how” (introducing the content of the question) and سزید (from سزا/سزایدن) is used in the sense “to be fitting/deserve,” so the clause asks “how (he) should be treated / what was fitting.”
segments.s474-g39: بدو is a dative clitic phrase here meaning “to him / unto him” (marking the indirect object). گفت is the past verb “he said.” کای is the vocative particle “O!” used to address someone directly, followed by مرد یزدان‌پرست “pious/God-worshipping man,” the person being addressed.
segments.s474-g40: که is a relative/conjunctive pronoun “who/that” introducing the subordinate descriptive clause. در کوه is the locative “on/in the mountain.” با غرم داری نشست is a compact predicate: با غرم (“with/harboring anger”), داری (“you hold/have” — present-tense possession/state auxiliary), and نشست (here verbal “sit” or perfective “have sat”); together they convey “who sits/has sat on the mountain in a state of anger.”
segments.s474-g97: نهم شب is a temporal adverbial ('on the ninth night'); Persian places such time phrases at the start. یکی گاو دیدم is a classical word order where the indefinite one/ certain (یکی) + noun (گاو) is fronted and دیدم ('I saw') follows; به خواب here modifies the verb and means 'in a dream' (the action took place in a dream).
segments.s474-g98: بر آب و گیا is a locative/descriptive phrase 'upon water and vegetation', with بر 'on/upon' governing both nouns joined by و. خفته بر آفتاب is a participial/adjectival phrase 'lying/asleep in the sun' (خفته = asleep/lying), placed after the locative for poetic rhythm.
segments.s474-g99: یکی خوب گوساله = 'a certain / a fair calf' (یکی = 'a certain', خوب = 'good/beautiful' used as adjective). در پیش اوی is an older phrasing for 'in front of him/it' or 'before it'; در + پیش = 'in the presence of' and اوی is an archaic/pronominal form referring back to the previously mentioned cow (equivalent to modernِ او/اش).
segments.s474-g100: تنش is 'his/its body' (تن + enclitic ش = 'its/his'); a string of adjectives follows the noun: لاغر و خشک و بی‌آب روی – 'thin/lean and dry and waterless-faced'. Adjectives are coordinated by و; بی‌آب is a compound adjective 'without water' describing the face (روی = 'face').
segments.s474-g101: همی is the classical progressive/continuative particle ('kept / was continually'); شیر خوردی ازو literally 'drank milk from her' (خوردی here marks the action 'drank'); ازو ('from her/it') gives the source and is then clarified by ماده گاو (the next phrase). Word order is verb + object + source, with the source optionally apposited by a noun phrase after the verb.
segments.s474-g102: This short clause names/characterizes the animal(s): کلان گاو is 'a large / full-grown cow' and then گوساله بی زور و تاو describes the calf as 'without strength and radiance'. کلان is an adjective placed before the noun (poetic ordering); بی + noun is the classical negative 'without ...' construction; تاو is a poetic word for brightness/radiance.
segments.s474-g103: اگر گوش داری is an idiom 'if you have an ear' = 'if you are willing to listen' (گوش here means attention). The following به خواب دهم ('I will give it in a dream / I will present it as a dream') uses دهم (I give — present-subjunctive) to express an action to be done (future/promise) and به خواب indicates the manner/medium ('in a dream').
segments.s474-g104: نرنجی is a polite negative imperative 'do not be angry'. همی is a small emphatic/continuative particle (here softening the request). تا introduces purpose/result ('so that / in order that'); ب + دین in the surface text forms بدین ('to/for this' — an archaic demonstrative construction; the separate word دین in the provided list does not match the listed senses so it is marked -1). سر here means 'secret / inner thought' (poetic sense), so the clause promises to give/reveal the matter to this head/mind. (Note: the giving verb دهم appears in the sentence's surface but is not in the provided word-list for this segment.)
segments.s474-g25: نشستنش is the verbal noun/masdar نشستن plus the enclitic -ش (his/its), so it means “his sitting” or “the way he sat.” The phrase با غرم و آهو is an instrumental/qualitative phrase introduced by با (“with”), listing two qualities joined by و. بود is the past copula (“was”), so the whole segment says “his sitting/pose was with X and Y.”
segments.s474-g26: ز آزار مردم is a prepositional phrase (ز = “from/because of”) indicating source or object avoided; مردم is the noun “people” marked by آزار (“harm/annoyance”). به یکسو بود is a state expression: به یکسو (to/at one side, i.e. apart) + بود (past copula) meaning “was apart/kept to one side.” Together: “he was apart from harming the people.”
segments.s474-g27: ز چیزی به گیتی is a circumstantial phrase meaning “from anything in the world” (ز = poetic 'from'; به گیتی = 'in the world'). نیابد is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive form with negative prefix ن- (here negative present: “does not/shall not find”), and گزند is the object 'harm'. Word order places the oblique phrase before the verb, yielding “from anything in the world he finds no harm.”
segments.s474-g28: پرستنده is a participle/noun meaning “one who venerates/admires” or “worshipper.” Here it governs two coordinated objects: مردی (abstract 'manliness' or 'manly virtue') and بختی بلند (a possessive/nominal phrase 'lofty/ high fortune'). The clause is verbless (appositive/descriptive) and describes the person who, as stated before, finds no harm.
segments.s474-g29: مرین here functions as a vocative (a proper name addressed: “Marin”). خوابها را is the definite object marker construction “the dreams” (را marks the direct object). به جز ... پیش اوی is the idiom ‘except before him’ (به جز = except; پیش اوی = in his presence), so the segment means “Marin, do not tell the dreams to anyone except in his presence.”
segments.s474-g30: مگو is the simple negative imperative 'do not say.' The second clause, ز نادان گزارش مجوی, uses ز (poetic 'from') + نادان ('the ignorant') and مجوی (negative/subjunctive imperative 'do not seek'); گزارش is the object 'report/news.' Word order is standard: verb imperative + coordinating و + prepositional phrase + object + negative-subjunctive verb: 'do not speak, and do not seek a report from the ignorant.'
segments.s474-g31: چنین گفت is a formulaic introductory clause 'thus said' (چنین = thus/so; گفت here functions as the past-speech verb 'said' or the speech-noun in Classical usage). با دانشی is an instrumental/attendant phrase 'with knowledge.' کید شاه reads as a genitive unit (کیدِ شاه) meaning 'the king's plot/cunning' or, by extension, 'the king's crafty man' — grammatically it's a noun phrase in apposition to the speaker. The phrase therefore describes who speaks and with what disposition.
segments.s474-g32: کزین = ک+ از + این (poetic contraction) 'from this/thereof'; پرهنر is a compound meaning 'full of craft/skill' or 'skilled'; بگذری is the 2nd/3rd-person present/future subjunctive/indicative stem of گذرْدن 'to pass' used here as 'to pass/overcome'; نیست راه literally 'there is no way' (نیست = 'is not'; راه = 'way/path'). Together they form the quoted judgment: 'There is no way to pass beyond this craft.'
segments.s474-g9: This is a locative prepositional phrase plus subject: 'به' marks the place ('to/into' ≈ 'in'), followed by the place name 'هندوستان'. 'هرک' (a poetic variant of هرکه) means 'whoever/everyone who', and 'دانا' is an adjective 'learned'. 'بدند' is the archaic past plural of بودن (they were) and completes the clause: 'in India, whoever was learned'.
segments.s474-g10: Parallel predicate to the previous clause: 'به' here has the sense 'in/regarding' (’in speech and knowledge’), 'گفتار و دانش' are coordinated nouns 'speech and knowledge', 'توانا' is an adjective 'capable/skillful', and again 'بدند' is past plural 'they were' — so the clause states that such people were able/skilled in speech and learning.
segments.s474-g11: 'بفرمود' is an honorific past verb meaning 'he commanded/said' (polite/epic register). 'تا' introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that/that', and 'ساختند' is past plural 'they built/made'; 'انجمن' is the object 'assembly/council'. The sequence means 'he ordered that they form an assembly'.
segments.s474-g12: 'هرانکس' (= هرانکس/هران کسی) is 'whoever/anyone', 'که' is the relative 'who/that', 'دانا' 'wise/learned'. The following 'بد' is an archaic poetic form of the past of بودن (بُد, 'was'); it links as the verb 'who was learned'. 'و' 'and', 'رای‌زن' is a compound noun 'counselor/advisor'.
segments.s474-g13: 'همه خوابها' is the direct object 'all the dreams'. 'پیش' here means 'in the presence of/before', 'ایشان' is the polite/third-person plural pronoun 'them', and 'بگفت' is the archaic past 'he said / he told'. Word order places the object and locative before the verb in classical style: 'he told all the dreams before them'.
segments.s474-g14: This is an imperative/report clause: 'نهفته' 'hidden' (adjective), 'پدید' 'manifest/visible', and 'آورید' is 2nd person plural imperative of آوردن 'bring/cause' (here 'bring to light' or 'reveal'). 'از' is the preposition 'from', and 'نهفت' is a noun/adjective 'that which is concealed'. Together it means 'Bring forth (make manifest) the hidden from concealment.'
segments.s474-g15: 'کس' here means 'no one' (negative sense), 'آن را' marks 'that (thing)' as the definite object, 'گزارش' is the noun 'report', and the verb cluster 'ندانست کرد' is an archaic/periphrastic way of saying 'did not know/do' — effectively 'no one reported it' (literally 'no one knew to report it / no one did report it'). 'کرد' is the light verb 'to do' attached to 'گزارش'.
segments.s474-g16: 'پراندیشه' is an adjective meaning 'full of thought / worried/careworn', 'شدشان' is past plural 'it became to them' = 'they became', followed by the coordinated body parts 'دل و روی' ('heart and face') and the adjective 'زرد' meaning 'pale/wan'. Altogether: 'their hearts became anxious and their faces pale.'
segments.s474-g105: یکی چشمه دیدم = 'I saw a certain spring.' یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (poetic), چشمه is the noun 'spring,' and دیدم is the first-person perfect 'I saw.' The prepositional phrase به دشتی فراخ follows the verb phrase and gives location: به + دشتی (plain, here in the adjectival form) + فراخ (spacious) — 'in a wide plain.' Note the object/locative may appear before or after the verb in poetry; here the locative follows.
segments.s474-g106: وزو بر زبر برده ایوان و کاخ = 'from it / by it the iwan and palace have been carried onto the upper bank.' وزو functions as a source-expression ('from it' / 'by it') though the lemma list does not give that meaning here, so its exact lexical sense is not in the provided senses. بر + زبر = 'onto/upon the upper (bank or surface)'; برده is the perfect participle/past form of بردن used resultatively ('had carried' / 'was carried'), and ایوان و کاخ are the affected things (objects) following the participle.
segments.s474-g107: همه دشت یکسر پر از آب و نم = 'the whole plain entirely full of water and moisture.' همه دشت = 'the whole plain' (همه as 'entire'), یکسر is an adverb 'completely/utterly,' and پر از is the idiomatic construction 'full of' governing the following nouns آب و نم ('water and moisture'). Word order: noun phrase + adverb + adjective phrase.
segments.s474-g108: ز خشکی لب چشمه گشته دژم = 'from the dryness the lip/brink of the spring had become sullen.' ز (poetic form of از) marks cause ('from/because of'), خشکی is 'dryness,' and لب چشمه is a genitive noun phrase 'the brink of the spring.' گشته is a perfect participle meaning 'has become / turned (into),' and دژم is the predicate adjective 'sullen/grim.'
segments.s474-g109: سزد‌گر تو پاسخ بگویی نهان = 'If it befits / if you deem it right that you answer secretly.' سزد‌گر is an archaic/formal expression meaning 'it is fitting' or 'if it befit,' تو is the pronoun 'you,' پاسخ is the noun 'answer,' بگویی is the subjunctive/present-oriented form 'that you say/answer' (the verb appears in a jussive/subjunctive sense), and نهان is an adverb/adjective 'secretly/hidden.'
segments.s474-g110: کزین پس چه خواهد بدن در جهان = 'from this on / henceforth what will the body desire in the world?' کزین = 'from this / hence,' پس = 'after/then' functioning adverbially, چه = interrogative 'what,' خواهد is the modal/auxiliary meaning 'will / desires' (future or habitual desire), بدن is 'the body,' and در جهان 'in the world' is a locative phrase. The clause asks about future/desirable aims of the body after the stated event.
segments.s474-g49: گذشتی is the 2nd-person singular past tense ‘you passed’. ز is the poetic form of the preposition از ‘from’. سوراخ پیل ژیان is a noun phrase in which پیل ‘elephant’ is qualified by ژیان (literally ‘life’/‘living’) — here functioning adjectivally/genitival to yield ‘the (living) elephant’s hole’ or ‘the elephant-sized hole’; the whole phrase is the object of the preposition.
segments.s474-g50: تنش را is a definite, possessive noun phrase ‘his body’ marked by را as a definite/direct-object marker (here topical/emphatic). ز تنگی is an ablative/causal phrase ‘because of/from the narrowness/constriction’. نکردی is the 2nd-person past negative ‘you did not do/you did not cause’; زیان is the resulting noun ‘harm/loss’ and follows the verb in poetic word order (verb + object).
segments.s474-g51: ز روزن گذشتی begins with z (poetic از) + روزن ‘opening/window’ followed by گذشتی ‘you passed’. تن و بوم اوی is a coordinated noun phrase ‘his body and his dwelling’ with اوی ‘his/its’ referring back to the elephant; in the poem this phrase contrasts with what follows (something is left behind).
segments.s474-g52: بماندی is a past-form verb meaning ‘remained’ (poetic inflection). بدان خانه is a locative phrase ‘in/at that house’ (بدان = به + دان, an older/poetic form). خرطوم اوی ‘his trunk’ is the noun phrase that the verb relates to; word order is inverted for poetic effect (verb — locative — noun).
segments.s474-g53: دگر شب ‘another night’ (دگر = ‘other/another’). بدان گونه is a fixed phrase ‘in that way/manner’ (بدان is the poetic form ‘به + آن’). دیدم is 1st-person past ‘I saw’; که introduces the subordinate clause that follows (that…). تخت is the subject of the subordinate clause (the throne).
segments.s474-g54: تهی ماندی is a predicate: تهی ‘empty’ + ماندی (poetic past ‘remained’), giving ‘remained empty’. از من is a genitive/ablative complement ‘of/from me’. ای نیک‌بخت is a vocative: ای marks direct address and نیک‌بخت ‘fortunate one’ is the addressee; the vocative follows the clause as a direct address to the hearer/reader.
segments.s474-g55: کیی here is the ethnonym/proper name ‘Kayanian’ (a reference to the Kayanid rulers); in context it functions as the interrogative/nominal subject ‘which Kayanian’. برنشستی is 2nd-person past ‘you sat (upon)’. بران تخت عاج is the locative phrase ‘upon that ivory throne’ (بران = بر + ان ‘on that’; عاج = ‘ivory’).
segments.s474-g56: به سر ‘on the head’ is the locative phrase marking where something is placed. بر نهادی is a verbal cluster meaning ‘you placed upon’ (نهادی = past of نهادن ‘to place’; here the verbal idea is ‘placed on’). دل‌افروز تاج is the object: دل‌افروز ‘heart-delighting, pleasing’ modifies تاج ‘crown’. Word order in poetry places the verb before the object for emphasis.
segments.s474-g81: رسیده is a past participle/predicative ('has reached') placed before the following prepositional phrase. به لب جان = 'to/at the lip(s) of life' (literally 'to the lip of the soul'), i.e. death is at the lips; ناتن‌درست is a postpositive adjective-compound describing the sick person ('unsound in body'). In classical Persian poetry adjectives or compounds often follow the noun they modify.
segments.s474-g82: همه چاره is 'all the remedy(s)/every expedient' and functions as the object/thing sought; تن‌درستان is the group from whom help is sought ('the body-makers' → physicians/healers). بجست is the classical past of جستن with an initial b- (common in older Persian) meaning 'sought'. Word order is typical: object/phrase + agent + verb in a compact poetic line.
segments.s474-g83: چو here = 'when' introducing a temporal clause. نیمی ز ... شب = 'half of the ... night' (نیمی 'half' + ز 'of' + شب 'night'); اندر گذشت = 'had passed/elapsed', with اندر 'in/within' and گذشت the past 'passed' (poetic past-tense phrasing). The clause overall gives the time-setting 'when half the night had gone by.'
segments.s474-g84: جهنده is a present-participial noun 'a leaper' or 'leaping one.' یکی باره is the classical two-word form of یکباره ('at once; suddenly') functioning adverbially. دیدم is first-person past 'I saw', and به دشت marks the location 'in/to the plain' (به + دشت). The sequence is literal: 'a leaping one, suddenly I saw in the plain.'
segments.s474-g85: A simple enumerative nominal phrase: دو + noun repeated for each body-part, joined by و, followed by داشتی, the second-person past 'you had/possessed.' This lists the creature's parts: 'you had two feet, and two hands, and two heads.'
segments.s474-g86: به دندان = 'in/at the teeth' (به here marking location/target), گیا = 'plant/vegetation', نیز = 'also/even', and بگذاشتی is the classical past of گذاشتن with the prefixed b- ('you placed' / 'you had put'). Together: 'you had also put vegetation in your teeth.' Note the archaic b- prefix on past verbs in classical Persian.
segments.s474-g87: چران is a noun here (lit. 'grazer' / 'the grazing/chewing part'); داشتی is 'you had' (past 2nd person). از دو رویه دهن uses از + numeral + noun to indicate composition or 'made from two faces/sides of the mouth' (رویه = face/surface; دهن = mouth). Poetic style often omits the ezafe or uses compact noun phrases.
segments.s474-g88: نبد is the negative/existential copula 'was not / did not exist' (classical form), followed by the locative phrase بر تنش ('on his body' with the possessive suffix -ش = 'his'). جای بیرون شدن = 'a place for going out' where جای 'place' governs the infinitive بیرون شدن ('to go out/exit'). The clause means 'there was no place on his body for it to come out.'
segments.s474-g89: بهشتم is a locative/possessive fusion (behesht + -am) meaning “in my paradise”; it functions as an adverbial phrase at the start. سه خم is a numeral + noun (three + kham) with the usual Persian number–noun order. دیدم is the past tense 1st‑person singular “I saw.” ای پاکدین is a vocative: ای introduces direct address and پاکدین is the addressee ‘pious one/person.’
segments.s474-g90: برابر نهاده is a past participial/adjectival phrase meaning “placed opposite/side by side” (برابر + نهاده). بروی زمین is a fused classical prepositional expression (بر + روی → بر روی / بروی) meaning “upon/on the surface of” and زمین is the locative head “ground/earth.” The sequence is a postpositive locative describing where the jars stand.
segments.s474-g91: دو پرآب و خمی تهی lists three items by description: دو پرآب = ‘two (that are) full of water’ (پرآب is an adjectival compound ‘full‑of‑water’), و links, and خمی تهی describes the remaining one as an empty kham (خمی here marks the one jar). در میان is the locative phrase ‘among/in the midst,’ indicating these three are considered together.
segments.s474-g92: گذشته is a perfect/participial notion ‘having gone/passed’ or ‘gone,’ followed by به خشکی ‘into dryness’ (به = towards/into + خشکی = dryness). برو سالیان is an older/poetic sequence meaning ‘have gone/become dry for years’ where سالیان (‘for years’) is a duration adverbial; برو is the verbal element ‘go’ used here in the sense of movement into a state (classical poetic usage).
segments.s474-g93: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from,’ introducing the source. دو خم پر آب is a genitive/grouping ‘the two khams (jars) full of water’ (خم = jar; پر = full (of); آب = water). دو نیک‌مرد is the agent phrase ‘two virtuous men’ (numeral + noun).
segments.s474-g94: همی is the archaic/prosodic verbal particle that marks a continuous or habitual action in classical Persian (often used with past forms to give iterative/ongoing sense). ریختند is the past plural ‘they poured’; اندرو = ان + در + او (classical fused form) or more simply ‘in/into it/therein’ (here: ‘into it’); آب سرد is the direct object/phrase ‘cold water.’ Word order places the verb before the locative (typical in poetry) giving ‘they kept pouring cold water into it.’
segments.s474-g95: نه ... نه sets up a correlative negation ‘neither ... nor ...’. از ریختن is a verbal noun phrase ‘from the pouring/act of pouring.’ زین کران in classical diction is equivalent to ز این کران ‘from this side/edge’ (زین here is the fused poetic form ‘ز + این’ = ‘from this’ and کران = ‘edge/side’). کم شدی is a past‑tense resultative ‘was diminished / did lessen’; syntactically the clause denies any diminution resulting from the pouring. (Note: زین is here a fused function word; if not recognized in the supplied lemma senses, it functions as ‘from this’.)
segments.s474-g96: نه آن خشک را دل پر از نم شدی again uses the correlative negation. آن خشک را marks ‘that dry one’ as the topical/definite object (را). دل پر از نم شدی is ‘the heart became full of moisture’ (دل = heart; پر = full [of]; از نم = ‘with/ of moisture/dew’; شدی = became/was made — a poetic past form of شدن). The whole clause says that even that dry person’s heart did not become moist.
segments.s474-g73: This is a prepositional/causal list introduced by the poetic preposition 'ز' (from/because of). The nouns are coordinated by 'و' (and); 'داد' here is a noun meaning 'justice' or 'just acts', while 'دهش' is a nominalized act of giving. The sequence ends with another coordinated pair 'خرید و فروخت' (buying and selling) introduced by 'وز' (here a connective/variant of 'و' or a poetic particle), so the whole chunk lists the things (reasons) involved.
segments.s474-g74: 'تو گفتی' is an independent clause with 'تو' (you) as subject; 'گفتی' is the 2nd-person past (you said) but the supplied sense list lacked the verbal sense, so use -1. 'همی' is a frequentative/adverb meaning 'continually/kept on', placed before the clause it modifies. 'شارستان' (the city) is the object/topic, and 'برفروخت' is a past-tense verb (sold) with the verbal prefix بر-; word order is Topic + Verb in this poetic line.
segments.s474-g75: 'ششم' is an ordinal/adverbial marker ('sixth' or here 'next, the sixth vision') placed before the verb 'دیدم' (I saw, 1st-person past). 'ای مهتر ارجمند' is a vocative: 'O, honored chief' — 'ای' calls out, 'مهتر' is 'chief/headman', and 'ارجمند' is an honorific adjective 'esteemed'.
segments.s474-g76: The subordinating particle 'که' introduces what was seen. 'شهری' functions as 'the townspeople / of the city' (here a collective/people sense), followed by the archaic/poetic verb form 'بدندی' (3rd-person plural past) meaning 'they bared their teeth / they glared'. The clause ends with 'همه دردمند' (all sorrowful), where 'همه' quantifies the subject and 'دردمند' is an adjective 'in pain, grief-stricken'.
segments.s474-g77: 'شدندی' is an archaic/poetic 3rd-person plural past (they became/they began). It combines with the verbal infinitive 'بپرسیدن' (to ask/inquire) to mean 'they began to ask/inquire'. 'تن درست' is a noun phrase where 'تن' means 'body/person' and 'درست' is an adjective 'whole/healthy/sound' placed after the noun (poetic word order).
segments.s474-g78: 'همی' again marks repeated or continuous action ('continually/again and again'). 'دردمند' describes the sufferers/afflicted as a group. 'آب ایشان' is a possessive phrase 'their water' (آب = water), and 'بجست' is a past tense verb meaning 'sought' (note the archaic b- prefix). The word order is Adverb + Subject-adjective + Object + Verb in this poetic line.
segments.s474-g79: 'همی' marks habitual or repeated speech ('kept saying/asking'). The verb 'گفت' (here used as 'said/asked') introduces the direct question 'چونی' (how are you/how do you fare). 'به درد اندرون' is a prepositional phrase 'in/with inner pain' where 'به' governs 'درد اندرون' (pain of the inner self). Note that 'گفت' was listed with nominal senses in the metadata, so the appropriate verbal sense index is unavailable (-1).
segments.s474-g80: This is a verbless nominative/nominal phrase that describes the state: 'تنی دردمند' = 'a suffering body' (تنی 'bodily' or 'a body', followed by adjective دردمند), coordinated with 'و' to 'دلی پر ز خون' = 'a heart full of blood' (دلی 'heart', 'پر' postpositive 'full', and 'ز' = 'of/from' introducing 'خون' = blood). This kind of appositive noun phrase explains the condition asked about.
segments.s474-g41: به ژرفی is an adverbial phrase meaning 'deeply' or 'to the depth'; بدین here functions like archaic/poetic 'to this' (equivalent to به این), linking to خوابِ من 'this dream of mine'. خوابِ من is the object/topic 'my dream'. گوش دار is an imperative phrase 'have ear' = 'listen/pay attention'; دار is the verb 'have' used imperatively with گوش.
segments.s474-g42: گزارش کن is an imperative 'report / tell (it)'; کن is the (imperative) verb 'do' attached to گزارش. و connects clauses 'and'. یک به یک is the adverbial phrase 'one by one'. هوش دار is another imperative 'have awareness / be alert', parallel to گوش دار.
segments.s474-g43: چنان دان که is an exhortation 'Know thus that' (چنان = 'thus/so'; دان is the verb 'know' in imperative/commandive sense); که introduces a subordinate clause. یک شب gives the temporal setting 'one night'. خردمند و پاک are predicative adjectives 'wise and pure', describing the speaker at that time.
segments.s474-g44: بخفتم is a past 1st-person form 'I slept / I lay hidden' (ب- here is inseparable in the older verbal form), with the -م ending marking 'I'. بارام is an adverbial or proper-name element giving place or a name of the bed/spot (lexically obscure here). بی‌ترس و باک is a coordinated phrase 'without fear and without anxiety' (بی‌ترس = fearless; باک = concern/fear).
segments.s474-g45: یکی خانه دیدم is 'I saw a house' with یکی functioning as an indefinite determiner 'a certain / one'. Object (خانه) precedes the verb دیدم (standard SOV order: I saw a house). چو introduces a simile 'like/as', followed by the noun phrase کاخی بزرگ 'a large palace'.
segments.s474-g46: بدو اندرون is a locative phrase 'into/within it' (بدو = 'to it/into it'; اندرون = 'inside'). ژنده modifies پیلی and means 'ragged/tattered'; پیلی here is elephant-related ('elephantine' or 'of an elephant'); سترگ is an adjective modifying the phrase ('great/huge' in contextually intended sense, though the listed lemma senses differ). The noun phrase as a whole names what lay inside.
segments.s474-g47: در خانه is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the house' serving as the scene/topic. پیداتر is the comparative adjective 'more apparent/more visible'; از introduces the standard comparison 'than' (from). کاخ is the thing being compared to 'the palace'. بود is the copula in past tense 'was'. Word order places the locative before the predicate.
segments.s474-g48: به پیش is an adverbial 'in front / before' or 'facing forward'; اندرون repeats the locative 'the interior/within'. تنگ سوراخ is a noun phrase 'a narrow hole/opening' (تنگ = narrow; سوراخ = hole). بود is again the past copula 'there was'. The sequence gives location (in front of the interior) followed by existence of the narrow opening.
segments.s474-g57: چیدنِ واژگان: 'سه دیگر شب' is a temporal phrase ('three more nights') placed before the verbal clause. 'از خوابم آمد شتاب' is a poetic/elliptical way to say 'haste came from my sleep' — i.e. 'I awoke hastily.' Here 'از' marks source ('from my sleep'), 'خوابم' is the possessive noun 'my sleep,' 'آمد' is the past tense verb 'came,' and 'شتاب' (a noun) is used as the cause/quality that 'came' (a stylistic inversion instead of an adverb 'hastily').
segments.s474-g58: 'یکی نغز کرپاس دیدم به خواب' = 'I saw a certain fine kerpas in a dream.' Word order is subject-verb-object with the locative/ablative phrase 'به خواب' ('in a dream') attached after the verb. 'یکی' here is the indefinite 'a certain,' 'نغز' is an adjective ('delightful/pretty'), 'کرپاس' is the cloth, 'دیدم' is the 1st-person past 'I saw,' and 'به خواب' is the conventional phrase marking that the vision occurred in sleep/dream ('in a dream').
segments.s474-g59: In 'بدو اندر آویخته چار مرد' the old particle 'بدو' functions as 'to it/unto it' (a locative/dative-like prepositional element); 'اندر آویخته' is a past participle 'hung upon/attached' describing position; then 'چار مرد' ('four men') names what is hung. The noun phrase follows the participial clause because of poetic word order.
segments.s474-g60: 'رخان از کشیدن شده لاژورد' is a compact predicative clause: 'رخان' (here describing soft/supple garments or similar items) is the affected thing, 'از' introduces the cause ('from'), 'کشیدن' is a verbal noun 'the pulling/drawing' (action), 'شده' is a perfect/passive participle 'has become,' and 'لاژورد' is the resultant adjective 'lapis-blue.' Together: '(their) rakhān had become lapis-blue from the pulling.'
segments.s474-g61: 'نه' is the negative particle 'not.' Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: object 'کرپاس' + adverb 'جایی' ('anywhere') + verb 'درید' ('tore') + subject 'آن گروه' ('that company/group'). The sense is 'That group did not tear the kerpas anywhere.'
segments.s474-g62: Again 'نه' negates the clause. 'مردم' is the subject 'the people,' 'شدی' is the past form of 'شدن' ('became' / 'were made' in poetic usage), 'از' marks cause ('from'), 'کشیدن' is here best read as 'the enduring/suffering or exertion of pulling,' and 'ستوه' is the predicate adjective 'weary.' Together: 'nor did the people become weary from the pulling.' Note the slightly archaic/poetic use of 'شدی' for 'became.'
segments.s474-g63: 'چهارم' is 'the fourth (one),' placed first to introduce the next vision. 'چنان دیدم' means 'I saw it so/thus' (چنان = 'so/thus' modifying the verb), and 'ای نامدار' is a vocative 'O renowned one' addressing the addressee. The clause functions as a lead-in to the following explanatory clause.
segments.s474-g64: 'که' introduces the content of what was seen ('that…'). 'مردی' here is an indefinite noun 'a man.' 'شدی' is the past of 'شدن' used as 'became/was' in a descriptive sense, 'تشنه' is the predicate adjective 'thirsty,' and 'بر جویبار' is a locative phrase 'by/upon the stream' ('بر' = on/upon/at, 'جویبار' = stream). The clause therefore says 'that a man was/become thirsty at the stream' (i.e. stood by the brook, thirsty).
segments.s474-g17: یکی (yeki) = 'one / someone' used here as 'someone' or 'a certain person' who speaks. گفت is the past tense of گفتن ('said') and introduces the reported remark. با + کید is a prepositional phrase 'with/by the stratagem/cunning,' and کای شهریار is a title phrase: کای = 'Kay' (the royal title) modifying شهریار = 'king'. Word order is Verb (گفت) then an instrument/argument phrase (با کیدِ کای شهریار).
segments.s474-g18: خردمند is an adjective/noun meaning 'wise' or 'a wise man.' وز here is an archaic/poetic cluster best read as و + ز ('and from' / 'and of'), i.e. a connective that links to what follows. مهتران is the plural of مهتر (great men, elders); یادگار is a noun 'memento / memorial / remembrance.' The sequence reads as a nominal phrase describing someone as 'wise, and a remembrance/tribute of the elders.'
segments.s474-g19: یکی functions as 'a certain' or 'there is one' introducing a character. نامدارست is an archaic fused form meaning 'is famed / renowned' (نامدار + است). مهران is the proper name. به نام = 'by the name / called' (به + نام). The word order places the predicate 'is famed' close to the subject phrase.
segments.s474-g20: ز گیتی = 'from/of the world' where ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' and گیتی = 'world.' به دانش رسیده is a perfect construction: رسیده (past participle of رسیدن) with به + noun means 'has come to / attained knowledge' (دانش). به کام = 'to/into (his) satisfaction' (به + کام). Overall this clause gives results: 'he has reached knowledge and fulfilment.'
segments.s474-g21: به شهر is a locative phrase 'in/at the city'; اندرِش = اندر + ـش where اندر = 'in/within' and the enclitic ـش = 'his' (in his). خواب و آرام نیست is a copular negation: 'there is no sleep and no rest.' Note classical word order places locative and possessive elements before the negated predicate.
segments.s474-g22: نشستش is نشست + ـش: 'his seat / his sitting / his assembly/meeting.' به جز = 'except' (به + جز functioning as the exception phrase). با دد و دام نیست is a negated nominal predicate: 'it is not except with enemies and traps,' i.e. 'his gatherings are nothing but with enemies and snares.' The final نیست negates the existence/occurrence of any other kind of meeting.
segments.s474-g23: ز تخم گیاهای کوهی خورد is a poetic birth/origin formula: ز = 'from,' تخم = 'seed/origin,' گیاهای کوهی = 'mountain plants/herbs,' and خورد (here an archaic perfect) = 'came/was born' or 'sprang.' In epic diction خورد often means 'befell/was born of' rather than literal 'ate.'
segments.s474-g24: چو = 'when.' ما را is 'us' with the object/topic marker را. به مردم همی نشمرد: به مردم = 'among/as (people),' همی is the older habitual/progressive marker (similar to modern همیشه or indicating continuous aspect), and نشمرد is a negative present/habitual form of شماریدن/شمردن meaning 'does not count/consider.' So the clause means 'when (he) looks/treats us, he does not reckon us as people.'
segments.s474-g1: چنین (an adverb) modifies the speech-act and comes first for emphasis: 'thus/so.' گفت is the past of 'to say' placed before the agent (a poetic inversion) — here it functions as the main verb 'said.' گوینده پهلوی is a noun phrase 'the Pahlavi speaker' (گوینده = speaker; پهلوی after it qualifies the speaker as Pahlavi-speaking or in the Pahlavi language).
segments.s474-g2: شگفت آیدت is a light‑verb construction meaning literally 'a wonder comes to you' — idiomatically 'you will be amazed' (آیدت = 'it comes to you', second‑person object enclitic -ت). کاین is the clitic combination of که+این introducing the following clause ('that this/that'); سخن is 'speech/tale' and بشنوی is 2nd‑person singular present/subjunctive of شنیدن used in the subordinate clause ('that you hear').
segments.s474-g3: یکی introduces an indefinite ('a certain'). شاه بد هند را is a definite noun phrase 'the (ill/evil) king of India' with را marking the (definite) object/topic; نام + following word here functions as giving the epithet/name — poetically 'was given the name/epithet [کید].' Word order is compact and poetically elliptical: 'A certain king of India was called (by the) name ___.'
segments.s474-g4: نکردی is the past negative ('did not do'); in Classical/Persian narrative this reads as 'he did not [do anything] except...' — جز از introduces the exception ('except from/by'). دانش و رای form a coordinated instrumental/genitive phrase 'by knowledge and counsel' and صید specifies the domain ('of the hunt' / 'for hunting' or 'in pursuit'), so the whole clause says he acted only by knowledge and counsel in hunting.
segments.s474-g5: دل بخردان داشت is a possessive construction: دل (heart) + بخردان (of the wise; plural) + داشت (he had) — 'he had the hearts of the wise.' The coordinate و links to the next noun phrase (مغز ردان) which is parallel in form: مغز (brain/mind) + ردان (of the righteous/virtuous); here each nominal phrase names qualities or loyalties he commanded.
segments.s474-g6: نشست is used as a past verb 'sat' / 'there was a sitting' (here: 'they sat' / 'a council took place'). کیان ('the Kian kings' or the royal house) is the agent/scene, and افسر موبدان is a compound meaning 'the chiefs/diadems of the mōbeds' (افسر originally 'helmet/diadem' used figuratively for high rank, موبدان = mōbeds, Zoroastrian priests). The whole line describes a formal sitting/assembly involving the kings and the chief priests.
segments.s474-g7: دمادم is an adverb 'constantly/continuously.' به ده شب پس یکدگر is a temporal phrase 'for ten nights in succession' (به + number + شب = 'for ten nights'; پس یکدگر = 'one after another' / 'successively'), so together they mean 'night after night for ten consecutive nights.'
segments.s474-g8: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle 'continually/still.' خواب دید is the simple past 'saw a dream' = 'dreamed.' این شگفتی is the object 'this wonder/marvel.' نگِر at the end is an interjection/imperative 'look!/behold!' (a poetical address to the listener or reader), so the clause reads 'he continually dreamt this marvel — behold!'
segments.s474-g65: همی is a frequentative/continuative adverb here (‘kept/keeps’). آب ماهی is a noun phrase ‘water (for) the fish’ (آب = water; ماهی = fish) and برو functions adverbially/directionally with the verb (an older/poetic locative/preverbal sense ‘there/on it’). ریختی is an archaic past form (‘you poured’), so the whole segment means ‘you kept pouring (water) there for the fish’. Note the subject is 2nd person implied by the verb ending.
segments.s474-g66: سر تشنه is a nominal phrase ‘(the) thirsty head’ (سر = head; تشنه = thirsty). از آب is a simple prepositional phrase ‘from/out of the water’. بگریختی is an archaic past-second-person verb ‘you fled/ran away’, so the clause says ‘(your) thirsty head fled from the water.’ The sentence therefore sets a contrast between pouring water and the (thirsty) head fleeing it.
segments.s474-g67: جهان here refers to ‘this life/ existence’ (a poetic sense). مرد in the manuscript-form could be read as a verbal past (‘died’) or as ‘man’, but the printed senses offered do not include the verbal sense; syntactically the phrase treats جان/مَوْت-theme elliptically and is followed by و + clause. و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and’. آب از پس او دوان is ‘the water ran after him’ (آب = water; از پس = after; او = him; دوان = ran/rushed). The segment describes a dramatic image: life/this world ends and even the water rushes after him.
segments.s474-g68: چه is the interrogative ‘what’. گوید in classical Persian normally means ‘says’ (here the provided sense list is for a noun, so the verbal sense is not listed — treat as verb ‘one says/it says’), and ب + دین together form بدین ‘to/with this’ (the tokenization in the data splits them; دین as listed normally means ‘religion/debt’ but here is part of the demonstrative compound). خواب نیکی گمان is a noun cluster ‘this hopeful/good dream’ (خواب = dream; نیکی = goodness/pleasant; گمان = conjecture/expectation). The line asks rhetorically what one can make of such a hopeful dream.
segments.s474-g69: به پنجم is a temporal phrase ‘on the fifth (day)’ (به = on/at; پنجم = fifth). چنان is a deictic/adverb ‘thus/so’. دید جانم is a verb phrase with subject-suffix: دید = ‘saw’; جانم = ‘my soul’ (جان + ـم ‘my’) — word order here places the verb before the possessor noun phrase (poetic flexibility). به خواب is the locative ‘in a dream’. The whole means ‘On the fifth my soul saw thus in a dream.’
segments.s474-g70: که introduces a reported/content clause ‘that…’. شهری is the noun ‘a city’. بدی in the metrical/archaic line functions as a second‑person past verbal form (classical contraction corresponding to ‘you gave/surrendered’) — this verbal sense is not present in the provided sense-list so is marked separately. هم = ‘also/too’. به نزدیک آب is a locative phrase ‘near the water’ (به نزدیک = near; آب = water). Thus the clause reports the dream-content: ‘that you also gave a city near the water.’
segments.s474-g71: همه مردمش is the subject phrase ‘all its people’ (همه = all; مردمش = its people). کور بودی is a predicative construction ‘were blind’ (کور = blind; بودی = past/copular ‘were’ in poetic/archaic form). به چشم is an adverbial phrase literally ‘to the eye’ / ‘in the sight (of the eye)’ describing the kind of blindness. Word order places predicate after subject in normal Persian fashion but with the archaic past auxiliary بودی.
segments.s474-g72: یکی را is an object/topic phrase ‘a single one / anyone’ with the object marker را. ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/because of’. کوری = ‘blindness’. ندیدم is the speaker’s past negative ‘I did not see’ (ندیدم = I did not see). به خشم is a phrase ‘in anger/with rage’ (به used adverbially). The line as a whole says the poet saw no one (among them) who was enraged because of the blindness — i.e. none showed anger at their condition.

sentences.s474-s17: At that instant he set his foot (to mount/dismount), and at Mehran's call he rose from his place.
sentences.s474-s18: The sages went with him together so that the spahbed would not be sullen.
sentences.s474-s19: When the sovereign reached Mehran, he asked the knower how (he) ought to be dealt with / what was fitting.
sentences.s474-s20: He said to him, “O pious man, who sits on the mountain nursing your wrath.”
sentences.s474-s49: On the ninth night I saw a cow in a dream, lying on water and grass, asleep in the sun.
sentences.s474-s50: A fine calf stood before it; its body was thin and dry, its face lacking moisture.
sentences.s474-s51: The calf kept drinking milk from her — the mother cow — yet the full-grown cow's calf lacked strength and radiance.
sentences.s474-s52: If you have an ear (are willing to listen), I will give it in a dream. Do not be angry; I will disclose it to this very mind (head).
sentences.s474-s13: His way of sitting was with fierceness and with a gazelle-like grace; he kept himself apart from doing the people harm.
sentences.s474-s14: A worshipper of manliness and of high fortune finds no harm from anything in the world.
sentences.s474-s15: Marin, tell these dreams to no one except before him; do not speak, and from the ignorant do not seek a report.
sentences.s474-s16: Thus spoke, with knowledge, the king's cunning one: 'There is no way to get past this craft.'
sentences.s474-s5: In India, every person of learning was skilled in speech and knowledge.
sentences.s474-s6: He ordered that they form an assembly of whoever was learned and an adviser.
sentences.s474-s7: He told all the dreams before them, saying, 'Bring forth the hidden from concealment.'
sentences.s474-s8: No one reported it; their hearts became anxious and their faces pale.
sentences.s474-s53: I saw a certain spring in a broad plain, from which the iwan and palace had been carried up onto the high bank.
sentences.s474-s54: The whole plain was entirely full of water and moisture;
sentences.s474-s55: If you think it proper to answer secretly, (tell me) from now on what the body will desire in the world?
sentences.s474-s25: You passed through the elephant's hole; you did not cause harm to his body from the narrowness.
sentences.s474-s26: You passed through the aperture; his body and dwelling you left behind — his trunk, however, remained in that house.
sentences.s474-s27: Another night I saw, in that manner, that the throne remained empty of me, O fortunate one.
sentences.s474-s28: Which Kayanian did you sit upon that ivory throne, and place the heart-delighting crown upon your head?
sentences.s474-s41: The sick one's life had reached his lips; everyone sought all remedies from the physicians.
sentences.s474-s42: When half of the seventh night had passed, I suddenly saw a leaper in the plain.
sentences.s474-s43: You had two feet, two hands, and two heads; you had even placed vegetation in your teeth.
sentences.s474-s44: You had a grazing/chewing feature made of two faces of the mouth; there was no place on his body for it to come out.
sentences.s474-s45: In my paradise I saw three jars, O pious one; placed side by side upon the ground.
sentences.s474-s46: Two (of them) were full of water and one was empty among them; they had been passed into dryness for years.
sentences.s474-s47: From the two jars full of water two virtuous men poured continually cold water into it.
sentences.s474-s48: Neither did you decrease from pouring on this side, nor did that dry one’s heart become full of moisture.
sentences.s474-s37: Because of its justice and giving, and because of buying and selling, you kept saying that the city had been sold.
sentences.s474-s38: Next I saw, O honored chief, that the townsfolk glared — all sorrowful.
sentences.s474-s39: They began to inquire after a sound body; the afflicted repeatedly sought water.
sentences.s474-s40: He kept asking, 'How are you in the inner pain?' — a suffering body and a heart full of blood.
sentences.s474-s21: Listen deeply to this dream of mine; report it, and be attentive to each part, one by one.
sentences.s474-s22: Know this: that one night, wise and pure, I lay down to sleep — I slept (in) Baram, fearless and without anxiety.
sentences.s474-s23: I saw a certain house like a great palace; inside it there was a huge, ragged elephant.
sentences.s474-s24: In the house it was more evident than in the palace; at the front of the interior there was a narrow hole.
sentences.s474-s29: For three more nights I woke from my sleep in haste; in a dream I saw a certain fine kerpas.
sentences.s474-s30: Upon it were hung four men; their soft garments had become lapis-blue from being drawn (pulled).
sentences.s474-s31: That band did not tear the kerpas anywhere, nor did the people become weary from pulling.
sentences.s474-s32: The fourth I saw thus, O renowned one: that a man stood thirsty by the stream.
sentences.s474-s9: One said, 'By the cunning of Kay (King) Shahrayar — (he is) wise and a memorial of the elders.'
sentences.s474-s10: There is one famed by the name Mehran; from the world he has attained learning and reached satisfaction.
sentences.s474-s11: In his city there is no sleep nor rest; his assemblies are nothing but with enemies and traps.
sentences.s474-s12: He sprang from the seed of mountain plants; when he looks at us he does not count us among people.
sentences.s474-s1: Thus spoke the Pahlavi speaker: it will seem wondrous to you that you should hear this speech.
sentences.s474-s2: A certain king of India bore the epithet of guile; he did nothing except by knowledge and counsel in hunting.
sentences.s474-s3: He had the hearts of the wise and the brains of the virtuous; the chiefs (afsar) of the mōbeds sat (in council).
sentences.s474-s4: Night after night for ten successive nights he kept dreaming; behold this marvel.
sentences.s474-s33: You kept pouring water for the fish there, while your thirsty head fled from the water.
sentences.s474-s34: The life/world ceased and the water ran after him; what can be said of such a hopeful dream?
sentences.s474-s35: On the fifth my soul saw thus in a dream, that you also gave a city near the water.
sentences.s474-s36: All its people were blind to the eye; I did not see a single one enraged by that blindness.
